_**User (2026-03-06 16:11:26Z)**_

Consider this review feedback: xmlTagsRe can over-match across multiple tag blocks and remove real user text between them because the pattern is greedy (.*) and does not ensure the closing tag matches the opening tag name. For example, <a>1</a> hello <b>2</b> would be matched as a single block and strip out hello. Consider using a capture group/backreference for the tag name and a non-greedy match for the content so only a single tag block is removed at a time, and add a test that has multiple tags with user text between them to prevent regressions.

---

```text
     1→package spi
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"encoding/json"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→	"log/slog"
     7→	"os"
     8→	"path/filepath"
     9→	"regexp"
    10→	"strings"
    11→	"unicode"
    12→
    13→	"github.com/specstoryai/getspecstory/specstory-cli/pkg/spi/schema"
    14→	"golang.org/x/text/runes"
    15→	"golang.org/x/text/transform"
    16→	"golang.org/x/text/unicode/norm"
    17→)
    18→
    19→// xmlTagsRe matches XML-style tag blocks including their content, such as
    20→// <ide_opened_file>...</ide_opened_file> or self-closing <tag />.
    21→// These are injected by IDE tools/system prompts and should be stripped before
    22→// processing user message text for filename or display purposes.
    23→//
    24→// The regex has two alternations:
    25→//   - <tag ...>content</tag>  — matched with (?s) so . spans newlines; uses .*? (lazy)
    26→//     to consume the shortest content between matching open/close tags.
    27→//   - <tag ... />             — self-closing tags.
    28→//
    29→// Known limitation: nested tags with the same name (e.g. <t><t>x</t></t>) will
    30→// only match up to the first closing tag, leaving a dangling </t>. This is
    31→// acceptable because IDE-injected tags are not nested this way.
    32→var xmlTagsRe = regexp.MustCompile(`(?s)<[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*(?:\s[^>]*)?>.*?</[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*>|<[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*(?:\s[^>]*)?/>`)
    33→
    34→// whitespaceRe collapses any run of Unicode whitespace into a single space.
    35→var whitespaceRe = regexp.MustCompile(`\s+`)
    36→
    37→// stripXMLTags removes XML-style tag blocks (and their content) from s and
    38→// returns the trimmed result with normalized whitespace. This cleans up injected
    39→// IDE/system context that precedes the real user message text while preserving
    40→// word boundaries where tags appeared between tokens.
    41→func stripXMLTags(s string) string {
    42→	cleaned := xmlTagsRe.ReplaceAllString(s, " ")
    43→	cleaned = strings.TrimSpace(cleaned)
    44→	cleaned = whitespaceRe.ReplaceAllString(cleaned, " ")
    45→	return cleaned
    46→}
    47→
    48→// extractWordsFromMessage extracts up to maxWords from the message, handling various edge cases
    49→func extractWordsFromMessage(message string, maxWords int) []string {
    50→	if message == "" {
    51→		return []string{}
    52→	}
    53→
    54→	// Normalize unicode and remove accents
    55→	t := transform.Chain(norm.NFD, runes.Remove(runes.In(unicode.Mn)), norm.NFC)
    56→	normalized, _, _ := transform.String(t, message)
    57→
    58→	// Convert to lowercase
    59→	normalized = strings.ToLower(normalized)
    60→
    61→	// Replace special characters with their word equivalents
    62→	normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "@", " at ")
    63→	normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "&", " and ")
    64→	normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "#", " hash ")
    65→
    66→	// Remove all punctuation and special characters, replace with spaces
    67→	// This regex keeps letters, numbers, and spaces
    68→	re := regexp.MustCompile(`[^a-z0-9\s]+`)
    69→	normalized = re.ReplaceAllString(normalized, " ")
    70→
    71→	// Split into words and filter out empty strings
    72→	words := strings.Fields(normalized)
    73→
    74→	// Take up to maxWords
    75→	if len(words) > maxWords {
    76→		words = words[:maxWords]
    77→	}
    78→
    79→	return words
    80→}
    81→
    82→// generateFilenameFromWords creates a filename from words, handling edge cases
    83→func generateFilenameFromWords(words []string) string {
    84→	if len(words) == 0 {
    85→		return ""
    86→	}
    87→
    88→	// Join words with hyphens
    89→	filename := strings.Join(words, "-")
    90→
    91→	// Ensure no double hyphens
    92→	for strings.Contains(filename, "--") {
    93→		filename = strings.ReplaceAll(filename, "--", "-")
    94→	}
    95→
    96→	// Remove leading/trailing hyphens
    97→	filename = strings.Trim(filename, "-")
    98→
    99→	return filename
   100→}
   101→
   102→// GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage generates a filename based on the first user message content
   103→func GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage(message string) string {
   104→	words := extractWordsFromMessage(stripXMLTags(message), 4)
   105→	return generateFilenameFromWords(words)
   106→}
   107→
   108→// GenerateReadableName creates a human-readable session name from a user message
   109→// The name is truncated to 100 characters at a word boundary and cleaned of excess whitespace
   110→func GenerateReadableName(message string) string {
   111→	if message == "" {
   112→		return ""
   113→	}
   114→
   115→	message = stripXMLTags(message)
   116→	if message == "" {
   117→		return ""
   118→	}
   119→
   120→	// Normalize whitespace: replace newlines and multiple spaces with single space
   121→	name := strings.Join(strings.Fields(message), " ")
   122→
   123→	// Truncate to reasonable length (100 chars) at word boundary
   124→	maxLength := 100
   125→	if len(name) <= maxLength {
   126→		return name
   127→	}
   128→
   129→	// Find last space before maxLength to avoid breaking words
   130→	truncated := name[:maxLength]
   131→	lastSpace := strings.LastIndex(truncated, " ")
   132→	if lastSpace > 0 {
   133→		truncated = truncated[:lastSpace]
   134→	}
   135→
   136→	// Add ellipsis to indicate truncation
   137→	return truncated + "..."
   138→}
   139→
   140→// appendRemainingParts appends the remaining path components from parts[startIdx:] to the
   141→// result path, skipping any empty components. This is used when we can't canonicalize the
   142→// rest of the path (e.g., directory doesn't exist or can't be read).
   143→func appendRemainingParts(result string, parts []string, startIdx int) string {
   144→	for j := startIdx; j < len(parts); j++ {
   145→		if parts[j] != "" {
   146→			result = filepath.Join(result, parts[j])
   147→		}
   148→	}
   149→	return result
   150→}
   151→
   152→// GetCanonicalPath resolves a path to its canonical form by resolving symlinks
   153→// and correcting case. This is important on case-insensitive filesystems like macOS
   154→// where "/Users/Foo" and "/users/foo" refer to the same directory but would produce
   155→// different hash values.
   156→//
   157→// The algorithm works by walking the path component by component, starting from the root.
   158→// For each component, it opens the parent directory and reads its actual entries,
   159→// performing case-insensitive matching to find the correct casing on disk.
   160→// This ensures that the returned path matches the real filesystem casing, which is
   161→// important for consistent hashing and avoiding subtle bugs on case-insensitive filesystems.
   162→//
   163→// Note: This can be slow for long paths or directories with many entries, since it
   164→// requires reading each directory's contents.
   165→//
   166→// If a component doesn't exist on disk, the remaining path components are
   167→// appended as-is, making this function safe to use with paths that don't
   168→// fully exist yet.
   169→func GetCanonicalPath(p string) (string, error) {
   170→	// Ensure we have an absolute, clean path
   171→	p = filepath.Clean(p)
   172→	if !filepath.IsAbs(p) {
   173→		var err error
   174→		p, err = filepath.Abs(p)
   175→		if err != nil {
   176→			return "", err
   177→		}
   178→	}
   179→
   180→	// Resolve any symlinks in the path to get the real physical path
   181→	// This is critical for ensuring consistent path hashing across different
   182→	// ways of accessing the same directory (e.g., through a symlink vs directly)
   183→	realPath, err := filepath.EvalSymlinks(p)
   184→	if err == nil && realPath != p {
   185→		// Successfully resolved symlinks - update p to the resolved path for case normalization
   186→		p = realPath
   187→	} else if err != nil {
   188→		// Symlink resolution failed (e.g., path doesn't exist yet),
   189→		// continue with the original path - we'll still normalize the case below
   190→		slog.Warn("GetCanonicalPath: symlink resolution failed, using original path",
   191→			"path", p, "error", err)
   192→	}
   193→
   194→	// Split into components
   195→	parts := strings.Split(p, string(os.PathSeparator))
   196→
   197→	// Start with root
   198→	result := string(os.PathSeparator)
   199→
   200→	// Skip empty first element (before leading /) that results from splitting "/foo/bar"
   201→	firstPartIndex := 1
   202→
   203→	// Build path component by component
   204→	for i := firstPartIndex; i < len(parts); i++ {
   205→		if parts[i] == "" {
   206→			continue
   207→		}
   208→
   209→		// Open current result directory to read its contents
   210→		f, err := os.Open(result)
   211→		if err != nil {
   212→			// Can't read directory - might not exist yet
   213→			// Append remaining path as-is
   214→			return appendRemainingParts(result, parts, i), nil
   215→		}
   216→
   217→		names, err := f.Readdirnames(-1)
   218→		_ = f.Close() // Best effort close, error doesn't affect result
   219→		if err != nil {
   220→			return "", err
   221→		}
   222→
   223→		// Find the correct case
   224→		found := false
   225→		for _, name := range names {
   226→			if strings.EqualFold(name, parts[i]) {
   227→				result = filepath.Join(result, name)
   228→				found = true
   229→				break
   230→			}
   231→		}
   232→
   233→		if !found {
   234→			// Component doesn't exist, append remaining as-is
   235→			result = appendRemainingParts(result, parts, i)
   236→			break
   237→		}
   238→	}
   239→
   240→	return result, nil
   241→}
   242→
   243→// debugBaseDirOverride allows overriding the base directory for debug output.
   244→// When set, debug files are written to this directory instead of the default
   245→// .specstory/debug/ location. This is set via the --debug-dir CLI flag or
   246→// the debug_dir config option.
   247→var debugBaseDirOverride string
   248→
   249→// SetDebugBaseDir sets the override base directory for debug output.
   250→// When set to a non-empty value, GetDebugDir will use this as the base
   251→// instead of the default .specstory/debug/ path.
   252→func SetDebugBaseDir(dir string) {
   253→	debugBaseDirOverride = dir
   254→}
   255→
   256→// GetDebugDir returns the debug directory path for a given session ID or UUID.
   257→//
   258→// The debug directory is used to store detailed JSON records and debugging
   259→// information for SpecStory CLI sessions. This is shared across all providers
   260→// (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI) to maintain a consistent debug structure.
   261→//
   262→// When debugBaseDirOverride is set (via SetDebugBaseDir), the directory structure
   263→// is: <override>/<sessionID>/
   264→// Otherwise: .specstory/debug/<sessionID>/
   265→//
   266→// Example:
   267→//
   268→//	debugDir := spi.GetDebugDir("abc123-def456")
   269→//	// Returns: ".specstory/debug/abc123-def456"
   270→func GetDebugDir(sessionID string) string {
   271→	if debugBaseDirOverride != "" {
   272→		return filepath.Join(debugBaseDirOverride, sessionID)
   273→	}
   274→	return filepath.Join(".specstory", "debug", sessionID)
   275→}
   276→
   277→// WriteDebugSessionData writes the SessionData as formatted JSON to the debug directory.
   278→// This provides a standardized, provider-agnostic debug output alongside provider-specific raw data.
   279→//
   280→// The file is written to: .specstory/debug/<sessionID>/session-data.json
   281→//
   282→// Returns an error if the debug directory cannot be created or the file cannot be written.
   283→func WriteDebugSessionData(sessionID string, sessionData *schema.SessionData) error {
   284→	if sessionData == nil {
   285→		return fmt.Errorf("sessionData is nil")
   286→	}
   287→
   288→	// Get debug directory
   289→	debugDir := GetDebugDir(sessionID)
   290→
   291→	// Create debug directory if it doesn't exist
   292→	if err := os.MkdirAll(debugDir, 0755); err != nil {
   293→		return fmt.Errorf("failed to create debug directory: %w", err)
   294→	}
   295→
   296→	// Marshal SessionData to pretty-printed JSON
   297→	jsonData, err := json.MarshalIndent(sessionData, "", "  ")
   298→	if err != nil {
   299→		return fmt.Errorf("failed to marshal SessionData: %w", err)
   300→	}
   301→
   302→	// Write to session-data.json
   303→	filePath := filepath.Join(debugDir, "session-data.json")
   304→	if err := os.WriteFile(filePath, jsonData, 0644); err != nil {
   305→		return fmt.Errorf("failed to write session-data.json: %w", err)
   306→	}
   307→
   308→	// Log absolute path for debugging
   309→	absPath, _ := filepath.Abs(filePath)
   310→	slog.Debug("Wrote debug session data", "sessionId", sessionID, "path", absPath)
   311→	return nil
   312→}
   313→
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-06 16:12:25Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The review feedback is about the `xmlTagsRe` regex on line 32. The current pattern is:

```
(?s)<[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*(?:\s[^>]*)?>.*?</[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*>|<[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*(?:\s[^>]*)?/>
```

The problem: the opening tag name and closing tag name are matched independently. So `<a>1</a> hello <b>2</b>` could match as a single block `<a>1</a> hello <b>2</b>` because `.*?` (even though lazy) will match `1</a> hello <b>2` when the closing tag `</b>` is found — wait, actually with lazy matching, `.*?` should match the shortest possible. Let me think about this more carefully.
 
With lazy matching, the regex engine tries to match as little as possible with `.*?`, so when it encounters `<a>`, it immediately looks for the first closing tag `</...>` it can find, which would be `</a>`. So the lazy quantifier should actually work correctly here and match `<a>1</a>` as a complete unit, not bleed into the next tag.

But I need to think about edge cases — what happens with malformed XML where a closing tag is missing or incomplete? That's where the behavior might diverge from what the reviewer expected. The reviewer's specific example `<a>1</a> hello <b>2</b>` actually works fine with lazy matching — it correctly matches `<a>1</a>` because `</a>` satisfies the closing tag pattern. The real issue is that the regex doesn't validate tag names match between opening and closing tags, so something like `<a>text</b>` would incorrectly match. The issue is really about mismatched tags, though with IDE-injected XML that's usually well-formed. Since Go's regexp package doesn't support backreferences, I need a programmatic solution instead of relying on regex alone—either manually parsing the tags or using a function that captures the opening tag name and then finds its matching closing tag.

Writing a function-based approach... Refining the XML tag stripping logic...

The approach iteratively finds open tags, extracts the tag name, searches for the matching close tag, and removes both if found. It keeps looping until no more matched pairs are found, then cleans up whitespace.

But I'm realizing this could get stuck if there's an unmatched opening tag before a matched one. Let me think about a cleaner approach using regex to find and remove matched tag pairs more directly.

Refining the tag removal logic...

I'm realizing the current approach of removing just the opening tag when there's no matching close tag could cause issues—it changes the string indices and might not handle edge cases like malformed tags or user code examples with angle brackets. But actually, for the common case where IDE-injected tags are well-formed, the existing logic should work fine. The key concern is making sure tag names match properly, and the current implementation handles that.

Since Go's RE2 doesn't support backreferences, I need a programmatic solution instead. I'll write a function that scans through the string character by character, captures tag names when it finds opening tags, locates the corresponding closing tags, removes the entire matched pair, and continues scanning—while also handling self-closing tags. Let me check the existing tests to see what cases are already covered.
</details></think>

```text
     1→package spi
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"os"
     5→	"path/filepath"
     6→	"strings"
     7→	"testing"
     8→)
     9→
    10→func TestGetCanonicalPath(t *testing.T) {
    11→	tests := []struct {
    12→		name     string
    13→		setup    func(t *testing.T) string
    14→		wantErr  bool
    15→		validate func(t *testing.T, input, result string)
    16→	}{
    17→		{
    18→			name: "absolute path with correct case",
    19→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
    20→				dir := t.TempDir()
    21→				return dir
    22→			},
    23→			wantErr: false,
    24→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
    25→				// After symlink resolution, the path might be different
    26→				// (e.g., on macOS /var -> /private/var)
    27→				// We should verify that the result is a valid canonical form
    28→				// by checking that it resolves to the same location as the input
    29→				inputResolved, _ := filepath.EvalSymlinks(input)
    30→				if inputResolved == "" {
    31→					inputResolved = input
    32→				}
    33→				// Both result and inputResolved should point to the same location
    34→				// Compare them after cleaning
    35→				if filepath.Clean(result) != filepath.Clean(inputResolved) {
    36→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want %q (after symlink resolution)", input, result, inputResolved)
    37→				}
    38→			},
    39→		},
    40→		{
    41→			name: "relative path converts to absolute",
    42→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
    43→				return "."
    44→			},
    45→			wantErr: false,
    46→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
    47→				if !filepath.IsAbs(result) {
    48→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want absolute path", input, result)
    49→				}
    50→			},
    51→		},
    52→		{
    53→			name: "non-existent path appends remaining components",
    54→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
    55→				dir := t.TempDir()
    56→				return filepath.Join(dir, "nonexistent", "path", "components")
    57→			},
    58→			wantErr: false,
    59→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
    60→				if !strings.HasSuffix(result, "nonexistent/path/components") {
    61→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path ending with nonexistent/path/components", input, result)
    62→				}
    63→			},
    64→		},
    65→		{
    66→			name: "deeply nested directory",
    67→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
    68→				dir := t.TempDir()
    69→				nested := filepath.Join(dir, "level1", "level2", "level3")
    70→				if err := os.MkdirAll(nested, 0755); err != nil {
    71→					t.Fatal(err)
    72→				}
    73→				return nested
    74→			},
    75→			wantErr: false,
    76→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
    77→				if !strings.HasSuffix(result, "level1/level2/level3") {
    78→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path ending with level1/level2/level3", input, result)
    79→				}
    80→			},
    81→		},
    82→		{
    83→			name: "path with special characters",
    84→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
    85→				dir := t.TempDir()
    86→				special := filepath.Join(dir, "test with spaces", "special@chars!")
    87→				if err := os.MkdirAll(special, 0755); err != nil {
    88→					t.Fatal(err)
    89→				}
    90→				return special
    91→			},
    92→			wantErr: false,
    93→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
    94→				if !strings.Contains(result, "test with spaces") || !strings.Contains(result, "special@chars!") {
    95→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path containing special characters", input, result)
    96→				}
    97→			},
    98→		},
    99→		{
   100→			name: "path with unicode characters",
   101→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
   102→				dir := t.TempDir()
   103→				unicode := filepath.Join(dir, "测试目录", "テスト", "🚀")
   104→				if err := os.MkdirAll(unicode, 0755); err != nil {
   105→					t.Fatal(err)
   106→				}
   107→				return unicode
   108→			},
   109→			wantErr: false,
   110→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
   111→				if !strings.Contains(result, "测试目录") || !strings.Contains(result, "テスト") || !strings.Contains(result, "🚀") {
   112→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path containing unicode characters", input, result)
   113→				}
   114→			},
   115→		},
   116→		{
   117→			name: "case-insensitive matching on macOS",
   118→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
   119→				dir := t.TempDir()
   120→				// Create directory with specific case
   121→				testDir := filepath.Join(dir, "TestDirectory")
   122→				if err := os.Mkdir(testDir, 0755); err != nil {
   123→					t.Fatal(err)
   124→				}
   125→				// Return path with different case
   126→				return filepath.Join(dir, "testdirectory")
   127→			},
   128→			wantErr: false,
   129→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
   130→				// On case-insensitive filesystems (macOS), should return the actual case
   131→				// On case-sensitive filesystems (Linux), it depends on what exists
   132→				if !strings.HasSuffix(result, "TestDirectory") && !strings.HasSuffix(result, "testdirectory") {
   133→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path ending with TestDirectory or testdirectory", input, result)
   134→				}
   135→			},
   136→		},
   137→		{
   138→			name: "path with trailing slashes",
   139→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
   140→				dir := t.TempDir()
   141→				return dir + "///"
   142→			},
   143→			wantErr: false,
   144→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
   145→				if strings.HasSuffix(result, "/") {
   146→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path without trailing slashes", input, result)
   147→				}
   148→			},
   149→		},
   150→		{
   151→			name: "partially existing path",
   152→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
   153→				dir := t.TempDir()
   154→				existing := filepath.Join(dir, "existing")
   155→				if err := os.Mkdir(existing, 0755); err != nil {
   156→					t.Fatal(err)
   157→				}
   158→				// Return path where first part exists but second doesn't
   159→				return filepath.Join(existing, "nonexistent", "deeper")
   160→			},
   161→			wantErr: false,
   162→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
   163→				if !strings.Contains(result, "existing") || !strings.Contains(result, "nonexistent") {
   164→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path containing both existing and nonexistent parts", input, result)
   165→				}
   166→			},
   167→		},
   168→		{
   169→			name: "symlink resolution",
   170→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
   171→				dir := t.TempDir()
   172→				// Create a real directory
   173→				realDir := filepath.Join(dir, "real", "directory")
   174→				if err := os.MkdirAll(realDir, 0755); err != nil {
   175→					t.Fatal(err)
   176→				}
   177→				// Create a symlink pointing to the real directory
   178→				symlinkPath := filepath.Join(dir, "symlink")
   179→				if err := os.Symlink(realDir, symlinkPath); err != nil {
   180→					t.Fatal(err)
   181→				}
   182→				// Return the symlink path
   183→				return symlinkPath
   184→			},
   185→			wantErr: false,
   186→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
   187→				// The result should be the real path, not the symlink path
   188→				// It should contain "real/directory" and NOT end with "symlink"
   189→				if strings.HasSuffix(result, "symlink") {
   190→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, symlink was not resolved (result still ends with 'symlink')", input, result)
   191→				}
   192→				if !strings.Contains(result, "real") || !strings.Contains(result, "directory") {
   193→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path containing 'real' and 'directory'", input, result)
   194→				}
   195→			},
   196→		},
   197→		{
   198→			name: "nested symlinks resolution",
   199→			setup: func(t *testing.T) string {
   200→				dir := t.TempDir()
   201→				// Create a real directory
   202→				realDir := filepath.Join(dir, "actual", "target")
   203→				if err := os.MkdirAll(realDir, 0755); err != nil {
   204→					t.Fatal(err)
   205→				}
   206→				// Create first level symlink
   207→				symlink1 := filepath.Join(dir, "link1")
   208→				if err := os.Symlink(realDir, symlink1); err != nil {
   209→					t.Fatal(err)
   210→				}
   211→				// Create second level symlink pointing to first symlink
   212→				symlink2 := filepath.Join(dir, "link2")
   213→				if err := os.Symlink(symlink1, symlink2); err != nil {
   214→					t.Fatal(err)
   215→				}
   216→				// Return the nested symlink path
   217→				return symlink2
   218→			},
   219→			wantErr: false,
   220→			validate: func(t *testing.T, input, result string) {
   221→				// The result should be the real path, resolving all symlinks
   222→				if strings.Contains(result, "link1") || strings.Contains(result, "link2") {
   223→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, nested symlinks were not fully resolved", input, result)
   224→				}
   225→				if !strings.Contains(result, "actual") || !strings.Contains(result, "target") {
   226→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want path containing 'actual' and 'target'", input, result)
   227→				}
   228→			},
   229→		},
   230→	}
   231→
   232→	for _, tt := range tests {
   233→		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
   234→			input := tt.setup(t)
   235→
   236→			result, err := GetCanonicalPath(input)
   237→
   238→			if tt.wantErr {
   239→				if err == nil {
   240→					t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath() error = nil, wantErr %v", tt.wantErr)
   241→				}
   242→				return
   243→			}
   244→
   245→			if err != nil {
   246→				t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath() unexpected error = %v", err)
   247→				return
   248→			}
   249→
   250→			// Result should always be absolute
   251→			if !filepath.IsAbs(result) {
   252→				t.Errorf("GetCanonicalPath(%q) = %q, want absolute path", input, result)
   253→			}
   254→
   255→			// Run custom validation if provided
   256→			if tt.validate != nil {
   257→				tt.validate(t, input, result)
   258→			}
   259→		})
   260→	}
   261→}
   262→
   263→// TestSetDebugBaseDir tests the debug base dir override mechanism
   264→func TestSetDebugBaseDir(t *testing.T) {
   265→	// Clean up after test to avoid affecting other tests
   266→	defer SetDebugBaseDir("")
   267→
   268→	t.Run("default path without override", func(t *testing.T) {
   269→		SetDebugBaseDir("")
   270→		result := GetDebugDir("test-session")
   271→		expected := filepath.Join(".specstory", "debug", "test-session")
   272→		if result != expected {
   273→			t.Errorf("GetDebugDir() = %q, want %q", result, expected)
   274→		}
   275→	})
   276→
   277→	t.Run("override changes output path", func(t *testing.T) {
   278→		SetDebugBaseDir("/custom/debug")
   279→		result := GetDebugDir("test-session")
   280→		expected := filepath.Join("/custom/debug", "test-session")
   281→		if result != expected {
   282→			t.Errorf("GetDebugDir() = %q, want %q", result, expected)
   283→		}
   284→	})
   285→
   286→	t.Run("empty override restores default", func(t *testing.T) {
   287→		SetDebugBaseDir("/custom/debug")
   288→		SetDebugBaseDir("")
   289→		result := GetDebugDir("test-session")
   290→		expected := filepath.Join(".specstory", "debug", "test-session")
   291→		if result != expected {
   292→			t.Errorf("GetDebugDir() = %q, want %q", result, expected)
   293→		}
   294→	})
   295→}
   296→
   297→// TestGenerateReadableName tests the generation of human-readable session names
   298→func TestGenerateReadableName(t *testing.T) {
   299→	tests := []struct {
   300→		name     string
   301→		message  string
   302→		expected string
   303→	}{
   304→		{
   305→			name:     "Empty message returns empty",
   306→			message:  "",
   307→			expected: "",
   308→		},
   309→		{
   310→			name:     "Short message returned as-is",
   311→			message:  "Let's create a session!",
   312→			expected: "Let's create a session!",
   313→		},
   314→		{
   315→			name:     "Message with newlines normalized",
   316→			message:  "First line\nSecond line\nThird line",
   317→			expected: "First line Second line Third line",
   318→		},
   319→		{
   320→			name:     "Message with multiple spaces normalized",
   321→			message:  "Hello    world     how   are   you",
   322→			expected: "Hello world how are you",
   323→		},
   324→		{
   325→			name:     "Long message truncated at word boundary",
   326→			message:  "This is a very long message that exceeds one hundred characters and should be truncated at a word boundary to avoid breaking words in the middle",
   327→			expected: "This is a very long message that exceeds one hundred characters and should be truncated at a word...",
   328→		},
   329→		{
   330→			name:     "Long message without spaces truncated at exactly 100 chars",
   331→			message:  strings.Repeat("a", 150),
   332→			expected: strings.Repeat("a", 100) + "...",
   333→		},
   334→		{
   335→			name:     "Exactly 100 characters not truncated",
   336→			message:  strings.Repeat("a", 100),
   337→			expected: strings.Repeat("a", 100),
   338→		},
   339→		{
   340→			name:     "Message with tabs and mixed whitespace",
   341→			message:  "Hello\tworld\n\nHow\t\tare   you?",
   342→			expected: "Hello world How are you?",
   343→		},
   344→		{
   345→			name:     "IDE tag prefix stripped before real message",
   346→			message:  "<ide_opened_file>The user opened /Users/foo/bar.go</ide_opened_file>\n\nFix the login bug",
   347→			expected: "Fix the login bug",
   348→		},
   349→		{
   350→			name:     "Only IDE tags returns empty",
   351→			message:  "<ide_opened_file>bar.go</ide_opened_file>",
   352→			expected: "",
   353→		},
   354→		{
   355→			name:     "Multiple IDE tags stripped before real message",
   356→			message:  "<ide_opened_file>foo.go</ide_opened_file>\n<ide_selection>some code</ide_selection>\n\nRefactor the auth module",
   357→			expected: "Refactor the auth module",
   358→		},
   359→	}
   360→
   361→	for _, tt := range tests {
   362→		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
   363→			result := GenerateReadableName(tt.message)
   364→			if result != tt.expected {
   365→				t.Errorf("GenerateReadableName() = %q, want %q", result, tt.expected)
   366→			}
   367→		})
   368→	}
   369→}
   370→
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-06 16:12:39Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I understand the full picture. The issue is:

1. The current regex `xmlTagsRe` doesn't ensure the closing tag name matches the opening tag name
2. Go's RE2 regex engine doesn't support backreferences, so we can't fix this with a pure regex approach
3. We need a programmatic approach to match opening/closing tag names

Let me write the fix. I'll replace the regex-based approach with a function that:
1. Handles self-closing tags via regex (these are fine)
2. Iteratively finds opening tags, captures the name, looks for the matching close tag, and removes the block


3. Checks the test file to see what other stripXMLTags test cases exist
</details></think>

```text
     7→func TestExtractWordsFromMessage(t *testing.T) {
     8→	tests := []struct {
     9→		name     string
    10→		message  string
    11→		maxWords int
    12→		expected []string
    13→	}{
    14→		{
    15→			name:     "Basic sentence",
    16→			message:  "Create a CLI battleship game",
    17→			maxWords: 4,
    18→			expected: []string{"create", "a", "cli", "battleship"},
    19→		},
    20→		{
    21→			name:     "Less than max words",
    22→			message:  "Hello world",
    23→			maxWords: 4,
    24→			expected: []string{"hello", "world"},
    25→		},
    26→		{
    27→			name:     "More than max words",
    28→			message:  "Create a complex web application with React and TypeScript",
    29→			maxWords: 4,
    30→			expected: []string{"create", "a", "complex", "web"},
    31→		},
    32→		{
    33→			name:     "With punctuation",
    34→			message:  "Hello, world! How are you?",
    35→			maxWords: 4,
    36→			expected: []string{"hello", "world", "how", "are"},
    37→		},
    38→		{
    39→			name:     "With special characters",
    40→			message:  "Send email to user@example.com & notify #team",
    41→			maxWords: 6,
    42→			expected: []string{"send", "email", "to", "user", "at", "example"},
    43→		},
    44→		{
    45→			name:     "Numbers count as words",
    46→			message:  "Create 3 unit tests",
    47→			maxWords: 4,
    48→			expected: []string{"create", "3", "unit", "tests"},
    49→		},
    50→		{
    51→			name:     "With contractions",
    52→			message:  "Don't forget to test it's functionality",
    53→			maxWords: 4,
    54→			expected: []string{"don", "t", "forget", "to"},
    55→		},
    56→		{
    57→			name:     "With accents",
    58→			message:  "Créer une café résumé",
    59→			maxWords: 4,
    60→			expected: []string{"creer", "une", "cafe", "resume"},
    61→		},
    62→		{
    63→			name:     "Empty message",
    64→			message:  "",
    65→			maxWords: 4,
    66→			expected: []string{},
    67→		},
    68→		{
    69→			name:     "Only special characters",
    70→			message:  "@#$%^&*()",
    71→			maxWords: 4,
    72→			expected: []string{"at", "hash", "and"},
    73→		},
    74→		{
    75→			name:     "Multiple spaces",
    76→			message:  "Hello    world     test",
    77→			maxWords: 4,
    78→			expected: []string{"hello", "world", "test"},
    79→		},
    80→		{
    81→			name:     "With emojis",
    82→			message:  "Hello 😊 world 🌍",
    83→			maxWords: 4,
    84→			expected: []string{"hello", "world"},
    85→		},
    86→		{
    87→			name:     "Mixed case",
    88→			message:  "HeLLo WoRLD TeST",
    89→			maxWords: 4,
    90→			expected: []string{"hello", "world", "test"},
    91→		},
    92→		{
    93→			name:     "File paths",
    94→			message:  "Read file /usr/local/bin/test.txt",
    95→			maxWords: 4,
    96→			expected: []string{"read", "file", "usr", "local"},
    97→		},
    98→		{
    99→			name:     "URLs",
   100→			message:  "Visit https://example.com/page",
   101→			maxWords: 4,
   102→			expected: []string{"visit", "https", "example", "com"},
   103→		},
   104→	}
   105→
   106→	for _, tt := range tests {
   107→		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
   108→			result := extractWordsFromMessage(tt.message, tt.maxWords)
   109→			if len(result) != len(tt.expected) {
   110→				t.Errorf("extractWordsFromMessage() returned %d words, expected %d", len(result), len(tt.expected))
   111→				t.Errorf("Got: %v", result)

   113→				return
   114→			}
   115→			for i := range result {
   116→				if result[i] != tt.expected[i] {
   117→					t.Errorf("extractWordsFromMessage() word[%d] = %s, expected %s", i, result[i], tt.expected[i])
   118→				}
   119→			}
   120→		})
   121→	}
   122→}

   124→func TestGenerateFilenameFromWords(t *testing.T) {
   125→	tests := []struct {
   126→		name     string
   127→		words    []string
   128→		expected string
   129→	}{
   130→		{
   131→			name:     "Normal words",

   133→			expected: "create-a-cli-battleship",
   134→		},
   135→		{
   136→			name:     "Single word",
   137→			words:    []string{"hello"},
   138→			expected: "hello",
   139→		},
   140→		{
   141→			name:     "Empty words",

   143→			expected: "",
   144→		},
   145→		{

   148→			expected: "test-case",
   149→		},
   150→		{
   151→			name:     "Multiple consecutive hyphens",

   153→			expected: "test-case",
   154→		},
   155→	}
   156→
   157→	for _, tt := range tests {

   167→func TestGenerateFilenameFromUserMessage(t *testing.T) {
   168→	tests := []struct {
   169→		name     string
   170→		message  string
   171→		expected string
   172→	}{
   173→		{
   174→			name:     "Example 1: Tell me about my",
   175→			message:  "Tell me about my @battleship.py game.",
   176→			expected: "tell-me-about-my",
   177→		},
   178→		{
   179→			name:     "Example 2: Create a CLI battleship",
   180→			message:  "Create a CLI battleship game for me in Python.",
   181→			expected: "create-a-cli-battleship",
   182→		},
   183→		{
   184→			name:     "Short message",
   185→			message:  "Hello",
   186→			expected: "hello",
   187→		},
   188→		{
   189→			name:     "Empty message",
   190→			message:  "",
   191→			expected: "",
   192→		},
   193→		{
   194→			name:     "Only punctuation",
   195→			message:  "???!!!",
   196→			expected: "",
   197→		},
   198→		{
   199→			name:     "With special replacements",
   200→			message:  "Email me @ user@test.com & CC #dev",
   201→			expected: "email-me-at-user",
   202→		},
   203→		{
   204→			name:     "Trailing spaces and punctuation",
   205→			message:  "   Hello world!!!   ",
   206→			expected: "hello-world",
   207→		},
   208→		{
   209→			name:     "Complex punctuation",
   210→			message:  "What's the best way? Let's find out!",
   211→			expected: "what-s-the-best",
   212→		},
   213→		{
   214→			name:     "File reference",
   215→			message:  "Update the @index.html file",
   216→			expected: "update-the-at-index",
   217→		},
   218→		{
   219→			name:     "Code related",
   220→			message:  "Fix the bug in foo.bar() method",
   221→			expected: "fix-the-bug-in",
   222→		},
   223→		{

   232→		},

   236→			expected: "fix-the-bug-in",
   237→		},
   238→	}
   239→
   240→	for _, tt := range tests {
   241→		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
   242→			result := GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage(tt.message)
   243→			if result != tt.expected {
   244→				t.Errorf("GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage(%q) = %q, expected %q", tt.message, result, tt.expected)
   245→			}
   246→		})
   247→	}
   248→}
```
